entry.title: Re: Unmanaged RAP — Deep Insert, CUD Operations & Action Handling
entry.content:
Hi @sanjay22 , 

Just delete  "strict ( 2 ); " from Behavior Definition and Behavior Projection. 

Spoiler

unmanaged implementation in class zbp_i_flightbooking unique;

//strict ( 2 );

 

define behavior for ZI_FlightBooking //alias <alias_name>

//late numbering

lock master

authorization master ( instance )

//etag master <field_name>

{

create ( authorization : global );

update;

delete;

field ( readonly : update ) booking_id;

association _Passengers { create; }

action ConfirmTicket result [1] $self;

}

 

define behavior for ZI_BookingPassenger //alias <alias_name>

//late numbering

lock dependent by _Flightbooking

authorization dependent by _Flightbooking

//etag master <field_name>

{

update;

delete;

field ( readonly ) booking_id;

field ( readonly : update ) passenger_no;

association _Flightbooking;

}

entry.title: Re: Login Methods - PWD, SSO
entry.content:
Hello @lornanapper 

Since its been a while we assume you have found the answer already. But please let us know if you require assistance still.

For this topic, I've some appointments:

Users with "Null" on "Login Method" it's SSO by default.

The Partial SSO in instances that have IAS, it's configured in IAS itself, so in there you'll have a link to PWD users use to login in your SF, this link could be find under "Conditional authentication, if you've an IDP this link should be shared by them

Regarding the Login Method, this could be changed through Employee Import (mass update) or through Manage Login accounts (user by user).

Regarding the place to select the Login Method it'll depend the feature that you're using to create those users, if it's by integration, by import, by add new employee feature.

 

I hope this clarifies your request

 

Regards,

Giba

A couple of issues with this request.  First we don't really do single company specific CodeJams. If they aren't open to the whole community that's kind of too close to a consulting engagement. But putting that aside for the moment, how many attendees would be talking about from the single company?

Second issue is with the topics.  We offer a set list of CodeJam topics. ABAP Cloud is one of them.  Be we don't customize the content per requests.  The presenter might be able to demo a few things you listed, but they aren't necessarily included in the content of the existing CodeJam.

entry.title: Re: Pricing issue in the free goods delivery.
entry.content:
Dear @Amit66,

If the system is creating the free-goods subitem with item category TANN then free goods determination is working the remaining issue is pricing. In standard the TANN subitem is only free because the pricing procedure applies a 100% discount condition for free goods. If that condition is missing or not triggered the TANN line will keep a normal price and won’t be zero

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a376cd9ea00d476b96f18dea1247e6a5/c864b65334e6b54ce10000000a174cb4.html

What to check and typical root cause:

1. Open the Sales Order and select the TANN item

2. Go to Item Conditions and look for a 100% discount condition

    • If you do not see a 100% discount, the pricing procedure is the problem.
    • Note the Pricing Procedure name shown in analysis often a Y…

You are using a custom or country-specific pricing procedure that doesn’t contain or doesn’t trigger the 100% discount condition for free-goods items

Example (Italy): SAP documents that with pricing procedure Y23101 (Materials Italy) the 100% discount conditions are S100 / S200 (not D100). If those aren’t applied, TANN is valued instead of zero

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3483411

You fix this by adjusting/using the correct pricing procedure so the free-goods subitem gets the 100% discount:

In CBC:

  • Set Condition Types for Pricing SSCUI 101120 ensure the relevant 100% discount condition type exists/usable
  • Set Pricing Procedure SSCUI 101117 and
  • Set Pricing Procedure Determination SSCUI 101118 ensure your sales area/customer/document pricing procedure combination determines a pricing procedure that includes the free-goods 100% discount logic

More details below for example by clicking Open Documentation in CBC:

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: How I Loaded 1.2 Billion Records into SAP HANA Without Crashing the Server (Or my Sanity)
entry.content:
@Tano_B  Thank you for the thoughtful questions — and that's a fascinating architecture with BW orchestrating through a virtual HANA table via DPA. I'd love to hear more about how that performed at scale, particularly how the orchestration layer handled failure recovery.

On the control table question: you're right that MAX(timestamp) inside HANA on a local columnar table is extremely fast — that's one of HANA's genuine strengths. The difference in our case came from the fact that we were calling MAX() against the virtual table, not a local one. Because the virtual table sits on top of a remote source via Smart Data Access, that MAX() call doesn't execute inside HANA's columnar engine — it gets pushed down to the remote source, which in our case was not optimized for that kind of aggregate scan across 1.2 billion rows. The round trip cost was significant and inconsistent depending on source system load at the time the procedure ran. Moving the boundary tracking to a local control table eliminated that dependency entirely and made execution time predictable rather than variable.

On the delta size question — this is an important distinction worth clarifying. The delta load does in fact scan across the full 1.2 billion rows on every run, and that is by design. We are not only capturing net-new records since the last load. We are also capturing updates and changes made to records that were already replicated to the target in previous runs.

A record that landed in the target six months ago could have been modified in the source system yesterday — and our delta needs to find it, identify that it changed, and apply the update to the target. That means the scan boundary cannot simply be "records created after timestamp X" — it has to evaluate the full dataset for any change_timestamp modification regardless of when the record was originally created.

This is what makes the partitioning and BIGINT decisions non-negotiable even for delta runs. You are not moving 2-8 million records in isolation — you are scanning 1.2 billion to find them. The arithmetic overhead and execution plan operate against the full table on every execution.

For use cases where source records are truly immutable after creation you could narrow the scan significantly and the architecture would look different. But for any source system where historical records get updated — which covers most ERP landscapes — the full scan on delta is the correct approach, not a limitation.

Really appreciate the questions.

entry.title: Re: Customer credit management issue
entry.content:
Dear @Amit66,

Why your first invoice can exceed:

This usually means your current setup is effectively checking only after FI open items exist and/or the SD documents are not updating credit exposure so the order value isn’t counted before billing

SAP own troubleshooting list for credit control not blocking points exactly to:

  • missing Static check of credit exposure (step 010) in the assigned check rule and/or
  • sales item category not marked Credit Active and/or
  • document type not correctly set for Automatic Credit Control / Credit Group

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3617102

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3367948

What to change to stop the first invoice:

Check 1.

In SSCUI 102623 Define Checking Rules ensure the check rule you actually use contains:

  • Step 010 Static/Statistical Check of Credit Exposure this is the key to stop first invoice slips through

SAP Notes below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3694862

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3617102

  • Optional but recommended Step 020 Maximum Document Value and set it to 50,000 if you want a hard stop for any single document above the limit

More details from SAP Community:

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-q-a/credit-management-maximum-document-value/qaq-p/12381000

IMPORTANT: You generally cannot change SAP standard check rules 01/02/03

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3555470

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2880254

Check 2.

  • SSCUI 102137 Set Credit Limit Checks for Sales Document Types your order type must have Check Credit and a Credit Group assigned

SAP Note and additional information from SAP Community that can help below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3367948

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/understanding-sd-credit-management-integration-settings-for-troubleshooting/ba-p/12856200

  • SSCUI 101518 Define Automatic Credit Control maintain the combination Credit Segment + Risk Class + Credit Group with a reaction that blocks

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3363301

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3674047

Check 3.

If the item category isn’t credit active the order value may not count into exposure first billing can pass

  • SSCUI 102571 Determine Active Receivables Per Item Category tick Credit Active for the used item categories

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3617102

Summary:

Use Manage Credit Accounts ->Simulate Credit Check on the credit segment and simulate 60,000. It should trigger the intended block already at sales order stage

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/918bca53037f408f91a2295d04ac16bc/82af159fa1034d14851cea6a7b0e0c38.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/918bca53037f408f91a2295d04ac16bc/be5747120f22446dbaeabc02044722d3.html

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Customer credit management issue
entry.content: Hi! This is a common scenario in S/4HANA Cloud — the first invoice can bypass the limit. To block it, you’ll need to adjust your credit check configuration or risk category rules so that credit is checked at order creation, not just subsequent orders.

entry.title: Unexpected value in position currency
entry.content:
Hello,

we've migrated interest‑rate instruments denominated in EUR (local currency is PLN). In simple terms, these are the flows we manually entered in FTR_CREATE and fixed in TBB1_LC up to 31.12.2025.

TCODE:DATETRANSACTIONTRANS. CURRTRANS. AMOUNTVALUATION CURRVALUATION AMOUNTFX RATE:
TBB1_LC31.12.2020PRINCIPAL INCREASEEUR1 000 000,00PLN4 200 000,004,20000
TBB1_LC30.06.2022PARTIAL REPAYMENTEUR200 000,00PLN800 000,004,00000
TBB1_LC30.06.2022REALIZED FX DIFFEUR0PLN40 000,00 (200 000* 4,2 - 200 000 *4,0 ) 
GO-LIVE DATE01.01.2026NO ACTIONS IN SYSTEM     

Corresponding FX rates was entered on 31.12.2020 & 30.06.2022 into table TCURR thus in FTR_EDIT &TPM20  the flows look correct both in EUR and in PLN.  

We fixed all flows using TBB1_LC with up to 31.12.2025– and the system fixed everything correctly (the realized FX difference was also "posted" in TBB1_LC as a DBT). It's a simple instrument with only foreign currency valuation step in the position management procedure (no ammorization or security valuation).

However, when we try to run valuation in TPM1 for 31.01.2026, the system shows PLN amounts as if the realized FX difference never happened. In other words, we correctly have EUR = 800,000, but SAP shows the book value on 31.01.2026 in PLN value as 3,360,000 PLN, which is incorrect (it should be 4 200 000 - 800 000 = 3 400 000 PLN). The same is for TPM12 - BookValue seems to ignore that repayment was made at 4,0000 rate.

There are 3 lines in TPM13 on key date  = 30.06.2022.

Outgoing Payment - Position Outflow Installment Repayment to Be Paid (Liability)
Translation of Negative Purchase Value Derived business transaction
Forex Loss (Scheduled Outflow) Derived business transaction

Could you advise me what might be causing this issue?

entry.title: Re: Service Entry Sheet Performance period is not defaulted
entry.content:
Dear @Sudharmv,

What SAP confirms:

Since Public Cloud release 2308 (CE2308), if the PO has a single performance date (start = end) then in Manage Service Entry Sheets Lean Services the Performance Period facet shows only one date no from–to range. A range is shown only if a real range exists in the PO

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3365482

So in most cases the dates are copied but the UI intentionally displays them as one date when the PO doesn’t contain a true range

What to check in your PO:

Open the PO item and verify:

  • Start Date for Period of Performance
  • End Date for Period of Performance

If they are the same or effectively you maintained only one date the SES will show just one date by design

IMPORTANT: Even when you do have a range in the PO, SAP restricts the SES performance period to that PO range. If the actual service happened later the workaround is to update the PO performance period first then create/maintain the SES

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3655589

Summary:

If your PO item clearly has different start/end dates (a true range) and the SES performance period is still blank/not defaulted then it’s worth raising an SAP incident with PO+SES screenshots but that’s not what SAP Note 3365482 describes

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Performance Optimization for High-Concurrency
entry.content:
We are supporting a mid-sized law firm running SAP S/4HANA Cloud to manage finance, billing, and matter-related reporting.

In addition, the firm operates a public-facing website where clients can access case updates, invoices, and selected document insights via secure APIs exposed from S/4HANA. The workload is mostly read-heavy (case status, billing summaries, payment tracking).

During peak periods (e.g., major case announcements or month-end billing cycles), we observe Increased response time in custom OData services, Higher load on analytical CDS views and Noticeable database utilization spikes on SAP HANA

There are no dumps, but performance degradation impacts user experience both internally (Fiori users) and externally (client portal website).

I would appreciate expert guidance on the following:

In a Public Cloud setup, is it best practice to optimize primarily at CDS level (reducing joins, avoiding complex calculated fields), or should we redesign the architecture to decouple external read access?

For client-facing website integrations, would SAP recommend a side-by-side extension approach on SAP Business Technology Platform (e.g., CAP service with replicated data) to protect the S/4HANA core from traffic spikes?

Are there recommended patterns for handling high-concurrency read scenarios while maintaining clean core compliance?

Our objective is to maintain performance stability, protect transactional workloads, and stay aligned with SAP best practices for extensibility and clean architecture.

Looking forward to insights from those who have implemented similar external-facing integrations in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

Thank you.

entry.title: How to join a parameterized view?
entry.content:
The view:

CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW "q_UFTrans"
(
	IN  TableName     NVARCHAR(30),
	IN  UserField     NVARCHAR(30),
	IN  LangInt       int
)

AS
	
	SELECT T0."FldValue", T3."Trans", T0."Descr"
		FROM          "UFD1" T0
			LEFT JOIN "CUFD" T1 ON (T0."FieldID" = T1."FieldID" AND T0."TableID" = T1."TableID")
			LEFT JOIN "OMLT" T2 ON T2."TableName" = 'UFD1'
			LEFT JOIN "MLT1" T3 ON T2."TranEntry" = T3."TranEntry" AND T3."LangCode" = :LangInt
		WHERE T0."TableID"  = :TableName;

It works:

However, if I try to join that in the following code....

	SELECT T0."DocEntry", T0."GroupNum", T1."U_ShowPayment", T2."Trans" from "OINV" T0
	LEFT JOIN "OCTG" T1 ON T1."GroupNum" = T0."GroupNum"
	LEFT JOIN "q_UFTrans"('OCTG','ShowPayment',9) T2 ON T2."FldValue" = T1."U_ShowPayment" 
	where T0."DocEntry" = '18057';

... I get the error:
SAP DBTech JDBC: [7]: feature not supported: Parameterized view with unused parameter: q_UFTrans

What is going on here? How do I join with a parameterized view?

entry.title: Re: How to get the URL of the OdataService generated by CDS
entry.content:
When you develop CDS views and OData services in SAP Business Application Studio, the HDI container created for your development branch is not exposed as a public endpoint. That’s why you can’t “find a URL” for your service inside your personal dev container — it simply isn’t reachable until you run it through a local CAP server or an approuter.

This is expected behavior. HDI containers are isolated by design.

entry.title: Re: SAP S4Hana Public Cloud - Custom CDS View - Need Cred...
entry.content: In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, the credit limit you see in the Manage Credit Accounts app does not come from the classic FI‑AR tables. It is part of the SAP Credit Management (FIN-FSCM-CR) data model, which is separate from traditional AR master data. Because of this, the credit limit field is not exposed in the standard CDS views used for AR reporting, and it also does not appear in the Custom CDS View app by default.

entry.title: Re: Text in GOS note popup not cleared after calling cl_gos_document_service->edit_note
entry.content:
This is known limitation of the GOS note editor when it is called repeatedly in the same ABAP session.
The class CL_GOS_DOCUMENT_SERVICE internally caches the note editor instance, so when you call EDIT_NOTE multiple times, the previous text buffer is not cleared. As a result, the popup keeps showing the text from the first call. it’s how the GOS editor is implemented.

One thing you can try, it may work, If you wrap the GOS call in a separate transaction, try and revert in case if it work.

something like  " CALL FUNCTION 'Your_GOS_EDITOR_FM' STARTING NEW TASK .

entry.title: Service Layer endpoint /b1s/v2/Attachments2 - BadFormat error

import org.apache.hc.client5.http.impl.io.PoolingHttpClientConnectionManager;
import org.apache.hc.client5.http.impl.io.PoolingHttpClientConnectionManagerBuilder;
import org.apache.hc.client5.http.routing.RoutingSupport;
import org.apache.hc.client5.http.ssl.DefaultClientTlsStrategy;
import org.apache.hc.client5.http.ssl.HostnameVerificationPolicy;
import org.apache.hc.client5.http.ssl.NoopHostnameVerifier;
import org.apache.hc.client5.http.ssl.TlsSocketStrategy;
import org.apache.hc.core5.http.ClassicHttpResponse;
import org.apache.hc.core5.http.ContentType;
import org.apache.hc.core5.http.Header;
import org.apache.hc.core5.http.HttpEntity;
import org.apache.hc.core5.http.HttpHost;
import org.apache.hc.core5.http.io.entity.EntityUtils;
import org.apache.hc.core5.ssl.SSLContexts;
import org.apache.hc.core5.ssl.TrustStrategy;
import org.junit.jupiter.api.Test;
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

class TestAttachments2
{
	protected static final Logger logger = LoggerFactory.getLogger(TestAttachments2.class);
	
    private static final String TARGET_URL = "https://127.0.0.1:90000/b1s/v2/Attachments2";
    
    @test
    void testUpload()
    {
    	final Instant startInstant = Instant.now();
    	
        try
        {
			final HttpGet httpGet = new HttpGet(TARGET_URL);
			String name;
			
			name = "/tmp/test.pdf";
			
			File f = new File(name);
			
			byte[] byteArray = IOUtils.toByteArray(new FileInputStream(name));
			
			final HttpHost host = RoutingSupport.determineHost(httpGet);
			
			final MultipartEntityBuilder builder = MultipartEntityBuilder.create();
			builder.setMode(HttpMultipartMode.LEGACY);
			builder.addBinaryBody("file", byteArray, ContentType.APPLICATION_PDF, f.getName());
			
			final HttpEntity entity = builder.build();
			
			final HttpPost post = new HttpPost(TARGET_URL);
			
			post.setHeader("Accept", "*/*");
			post.setHeader("Cookie", "ROUTEID=.node3; B1SESSION=xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx");
			post.setHeader("Authorization", "Bearer xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx");
			
			post.setEntity(entity);
			
			try( CloseableHttpClient client = buildClient())
			{
				final ClassicHttpResponse response = client.executeOpen(host, post, null);
				logger.info("response.getCode(): {}", response.getCode());
				
				final HttpEntity repoEntity = response.getEntity();
				
				if (Objects.nonNull(repoEntity))
				{
					logger.info("\n\n{}\n\n", EntityUtils.toString(repoEntity));
				}
				
				if (response.getHeaders() != null)
				{
					for (Header item : response.getHeaders())
					{
						logger.info(item.getName() + ": " + item.getValue());
					}
				}
			}
			
			
        }
        catch (Exception e)
        {
        	logger.error(e.getMessage(), e);
        }
		
		assertTrue( startInstant.isBefore(Instant.now()) );
    }
    
    private CloseableHttpClient buildClient() throws KeyManagementException, UnrecoverableKeyException, NoSuchAlgorithmException, KeyStoreException, CertificateException, IOException
    {
		final TrustStrategy acceptAllCerts = (cert, authType) -> true;
		
		final SSLContext sslcontext = SSLContexts.custom()
				.loadTrustMaterial(acceptAllCerts)
				.build();

		final DefaultClientTlsStrategy tlsStrategy = new DefaultClientTlsStrategy(sslcontext,
				HostnameVerificationPolicy.CLIENT, NoopHostnameVerifier.INSTANCE);
		
		final PoolingHttpClientConnectionManager connManager = PoolingHttpClientConnectionManagerBuilder.create()
				.setTlsSocketStrategy((TlsSocketStrategy) tlsStrategy)
				.build();
		
		final CloseableHttpClient client = HttpClientBuilder.create()
		        .setConnectionManager(connManager)
		        .build();
		
		return client;
    }
}

 I use Sl4J and Logback for logging and bellow are the XML to configure the logging framework:

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<configuration>
	<include resource="org/springframework/boot/logging/logback/defaults.xml"/>
	
	<statusListener class="ch.qos.logback.core.status.NopStatusListener" />
	​
	<springProperty scope="context" name="springAppName" source="spring.application.name"/>
	<!-- Example for logging into the build folder of your project -->
	<property name="LOG_FILE" value="${BUILD_FOLDER:-build}/${springAppName}"/>​

	<!-- Appender to log to console -->
	<appender name="console" class="ch.qos.logback.core.ConsoleAppender">
		<filter class="ch.qos.logback.classic.filter.ThresholdFilter">
			<!-- Minimum logging level to be presented in the console logs-->
			<level>DEBUG</level>
		</filter>
		<encoder>
			<pattern>%d{dd-MM-yyyy HH:mm:ss.SSS} [%X{X-Trace-Id}] [%thread] %-5level %logger{36}.%M:%line - %msg%n</pattern>
			<charset>utf8</charset>
		</encoder>
	</appender>
	
	<logger name="org.apache.http" level="DEBUG"/>
	<logger name="org.apache.hc.client5.http" level="debug"/>
	​
	<root level="INFO">
		<appender-ref ref="console"/>
	</root>
</configuration>

entry.title: Re: The Only Script you would ever need for Message Logging and Custom Headers in SAP CI
entry.content:
Hello @sjorge 

Sure I understand the concern and will try to address them here

It only raises one concern from my end, which is in the scenario where you have a CI with a lot of iFlows; doing this for all of them would for sure consume a lot of memory from the instance.

Definitely true. The alternative was to capture payload values in property and then add them to custom header by hardcoding their names in groovy. which would have consumed more tenant memory (disk memory as well as runtime)

This Solution consumes same amount of runtime memory as extracting a property in content modifier via expression or xpath needs same runtime memory as needed by the groovy parser but this solution as its centralized will save you from multiple scripts saved in the tenant which are iflow specific.


Didn't you ever experience memory issues or performance issues due to a lot of the memory being consumed for logging?
How do you tackle this?

Actually no, I have not observed memory issues ever because of lot of logging but Custom headers aare anyways implemented for critical iflows only. I do not suggest to log the payload at every step that's why it has a option to make logger value 0 which would then not save the payload as an attachment.

From my personal experience 3-4 custom mpl headers across all iflows are very normal and wont hurt the memory.

entry.title: Unlocking SAP BODS .ATL Files: Stop Taking Screenshots and Extract Datastore Metadata with Python!
entry.content:
Introduction

If you work with SAP Data Services (BODS), you are intimately familiar with .atl files. They are the lifeblood of our migrations, backups, and version control.

But think about your typical environment refresh scenario. When you need to document connection details, do you really want to spend hours manually clicking through the BODS Designer, opening each and every Datastore and File Location just to take screenshots of their configurations?

Of course not! It is tedious, time-consuming, and highly prone to human error.

You might think, "I'll just export the .atl file, open it in Notepad, and search for the details." But when you open a large .atl file, you are immediately greeted by thousands of lines of code, nested brackets, and massive blocks of encrypted XML. Finding exactly which OData URL or SAP Host Name is tied to a specific Datastore is like trying to find a needle in a haystack.

Today, I am going to share a lightweight, plug-and-play Python script I use to completely automate this. It parses raw .atl files and extracts every Datastore and File Location property into clean, perfectly aligned CSV files.

 

The Challenge: Hidden XML and Adapter Properties

At first glance, extracting data from an .atl file seems simple enough using Regular Expressions. Standard Datastore properties are written in plain text (e.g., "application_type" = 'R/3').

However, things get tricky with Server Names, Host Names, and URLs. SAP BODS buries these inside a massive XML block called <DSConfigurations>. To make it even more complex, for Adapters (like OData), the URL and User credentials are encrypted inside a second layer of encoded XML (<adapter_dsparams_xml>).

If you just do a standard text search, you will miss your Cloud, FTP, and OData connection details entirely.

The Solution: A Custom Python Parser

To solve this, I wrote a Python script that systematically chops the .atl file into discrete object blocks, decrypts the hidden Adapter XML, and dynamically maps every single XML tag it finds into its own CSV column.

Because the script dynamically builds the columns based on the data it finds, you get exactly what is in your system (e.g., abap_server_dir, odata_service_url, fl_bucket, etc.) with zero misaligned data. It also adds a date-time stamp to your output files so you don't accidentally overwrite previous runs.

The Code

You do not need any special Python libraries to run this—just standard Python 3. Save the following code to a file named bods_parser.py:

 

import re
import csv
import html
import os
from datetime import datetime

def parse_atl_file(atl_file_path):
    print(f"\nReading ATL file: {atl_file_path}...")
    
    if not os.path.exists(atl_file_path):
        print(f"❌ Error: Could not find '{atl_file_path}'. Please check the name and try again.")
        return
    
    with open(atl_file_path, 'r', encoding='utf-8', errors='ignore') as file:
        content = file.read()

    datastores = []
    file_locations = []
    ds_keys = set(["Datastore_Name"])
    fl_keys = set(["File_Location_Name"])

    # Split by CREATE to cleanly process objects without guessing parenthesis
    blocks = re.split(r'\n(?=CREATE )', content)
    
    for block in blocks:
        
        # --- 1. Parse File Locations ---
        if block.startswith("CREATE __BODI_FILELOCATION DATASTORE"):
            name_match = re.search(r"CREATE __BODI_FILELOCATION DATASTORE\s+([A-Za-z0-9_]+)", block)
            if not name_match: continue
            props = {"File_Location_Name": name_match.group(1)}
            
            # Find XML tags holding the bucket, project, and directory info
            tags = re.findall(r'<([a-zA-Z0-9_]+)[^>]*>([^<]*)</\1>', block)
            for key, val in tags:
                if val.strip() and key != 'DSConfiguration':
                    props[key] = val.strip()
                    fl_keys.add(key)
            file_locations.append(props)

        # --- 2. Parse Database & Adapter Datastores ---
        elif block.startswith("CREATE DATABASE DATASTORE"):
            name_match = re.search(r"CREATE DATABASE DATASTORE\s+([A-Za-z0-9_]+)", block)
            if not name_match: continue
            props = {"Datastore_Name": name_match.group(1)}
                
            xml_match = re.search(r'<DSConfigurations>.*?</DSConfigurations>', block, re.DOTALL)
            if xml_match:
                xml_text = xml_match.group(0)
                
                # CRITICAL FIX: The URLs and Users are actually hidden in 'adapter_dsparams_xml'
                adapter_match = re.search(r'<adapter_dsparams_xml>(.*?)</adapter_dsparams_xml>', xml_text, re.DOTALL)
                if adapter_match:
                    unescaped_xml = html.unescape(adapter_match.group(1)) # Decode the hidden XML
                    xml_text += "\n" + unescaped_xml
                
                # Extract all properties dynamically
                tags = re.findall(r'<([a-zA-Z0-9_]+)[^>]*>([^<]*)</\1>', xml_text)
                for key, val in tags:
                    val = val.strip()
                    if val and key not in ['adapter_dsparams_xml', 'adapter_properties_xml', 'DSConfiguration']:
                        props[key] = val
                        ds_keys.add(key)
                        
            datastores.append(props)

    # Generate timestamp for the file names
    timestamp = datetime.now().strftime("%Y%m%d_%H%M%S")

    # Export to CSV
    if file_locations:
        fl_filename = f'FileLocations_Detailed_{timestamp}.csv'
        with open(fl_filename, 'w', newline='', encoding='utf-8') as f:
            columns = ["File_Location_Name"] + sorted([k for k in fl_keys if k != "File_Location_Name"])
            writer = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=columns)
            writer.writeheader()
            writer.writerows(file_locations)
        print(f"✅ SUCCESS: Extracted File Locations to {fl_filename}")

    if datastores:
        ds_filename = f'Datastores_Detailed_{timestamp}.csv'
        with open(ds_filename, 'w', newline='', encoding='utf-8') as f:
            columns = ["Datastore_Name"] + sorted([k for k in ds_keys if k != "Datastore_Name"])
            writer = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=columns)
            writer.writeheader()
            writer.writerows(datastores)
        print(f"✅ SUCCESS: Extracted Datastores to {ds_filename}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    print("Ensure only ONE .atl file is in this folder.")
    user_file = input("Please enter the exact name of your ATL file (e.g., target.atl): ").strip()
    
    if user_file:
        parse_atl_file(user_file)
    else:
        print("❌ No file name provided. Exiting.")

How to Run the Extractor

  1. Set up: Place your .atl export file in the exact same folder as your bods_parser.py script.

  2. Execute: Open your terminal or command prompt and run: python bods_parser.py (or python3 bods_parser.py on Linux).

  3. Input: The script will prompt you for the file name. Type it in (e.g., PROD_export.atl) and hit Enter.

In milliseconds, it will generate two timestamped files (e.g., Datastores_Detailed_20260224_143000.csv), giving you a complete, highly readable inventory of your environment connections. Say goodbye to manual screenshots!

What's Next?

 

Automating your repository documentation saves hours of manual checking, especially during large-scale migrations, environment refreshes, or internal audits.

If you found this script helpful, let me know in the comments! I have actually built an even more advanced version of this tool that extracts the entire ATL file—including all Workflows, Dataflows, Jobs, and Tables—into 9 completely separated, perfectly aligned CSV files.

If there is enough interest, I will write a follow-up post sharing that ultimate master script. Happy coding!

 

SAP Data Services Python #SAPCommunity #Automation

entry.title: Re: OCR for Grounding management
entry.content:
Preprocess Files with OCR Before Grounding.

  1.  Run OCR on images and scanned PDFs using:
    • SAP AI Core custom OCR pipeline
    • SAP Document Information Extraction
    • External OCR service (Azure OCR, AWS, Textract, Tesseract, etc.)
  2. Extract the recognized text
  3. Append or replace the original file content with the extracted text
  4. Then generate embeddings using Text Embedding 3 Large
  5. Store in your vector repository.

This ensures the embedding model works with real textual context.

entry.title: Re: Trial SAP BTP with Process automation are not maintained with Extract Data (Pre-trained Model)
entry.content:
Hi,

This is not a configuration issue - it's a regional limitation in the trial environment.

The "Extract Data (Pre-trained Model)" action depends on Document Information Extraction (DOX). In the trial account, SAP Build Process Automation is available only in Singapore - Azure, but DOX is not deployed in that region.

The template is still visible because templates are global and not filtered based on regional service availability.

Summary:

This is a known trail limitation.

To fully run the template, you need a productive BTP subaccount in a supported region (e.g. Frankfurt, US, East, Tokyo, Sydney).

For trial testing, you can mock the extraction output or skip the pre-trained block.

entry.title: Re: Initialization with spartacussampledata is taking mor...
entry.content:
HI,

I am able to initialize the system properly. 

  1. I followed the steps mentioned in this page to setup 2211 in my Apple Laptop (MacOS Tahoe 26.3, 2.3 GHz 8-Core Intel Core i9 Processor, 32 GB RAM with 500 GB Disk space). 
  2. The instructions mentioned in the above page is not helping when there are issues during the execution. The initialize command ./install.sh -r cx_features_enabled_for_spa initialize took 40 minutes to 2 hours at times. 
  3. when I ran the start command first time, it took almost 18 hours and it has been still running. ./install.sh -r cx_features_enabled_for_spa start. 
  4. I had to play around with different configurations and commenting extensions to narrow down the issue. I did the following configurations to make it work.
    1. I added mysql DB and then the performance has improved drastically.
    2. I was getting Connection refused error periodically during initialization and server startup. The installation guide doesn't have any instructions on how to resolve this issue. This issue looks like we need to configure Azure Blob credentials in the property file. Why do we need this to run SAPCC in locally? This error was a major reason for delay in initialization and server startup. If this is mandatory then please update your installation guide.
Suppressed: io.netty.channel.AbstractChannel$AnnotatedConnectException: finishConnect(..) failed with error(-61): Connection refused: localhost/[0:0:0:0:0:0:0:1]:10001
        Caused by: java.net.ConnectException: finishConnect(..) failed with error(-61): Connection refused
                at io.netty.channel.unix.Errors.newConnectException0(Errors.java:166) ~[netty-transport-native-unix-common-4.1.130.Final.jar:4.1.130.Final]
                at io.netty.channel.unix.Errors.handleConnectErrno(Errors.java:131) ~[netty-transport-native-unix-common-4.1.130.Final.jar:4.1.130.Final]
                at io.netty.channel.unix.Socket.finishConnect(Socket.java:359) ~[netty-transport-native-unix-common-4.1.130.Final.jar:4.1.130.Final]
                at io.netty.channel.kqueue.AbstractKQueueChannel$AbstractKQueueUnsafe.doFinishConnect(AbstractKQueueChannel.java:673) ~[netty-transport-classes-kqueue-4.1.130.Final.jar:4.1.130.Final]

                at io.netty.channel.kqueue.AbstractKQueueChannel$AbstractKQueueUnsafe.readEOF(AbstractKQueueChannel.java:496) ~[netty-transport-classes-kqueue-4.1.130.Final.jar:4.1.130.Final]

    // integration-apis
    // extName 'inboundservices'

    / # Don't attempt geocoding POS addresses
    property 'commerceservices.geocoding.batch.enabled','false'
  3. Reinstallation is failing because the bin/custom directory contains yb2bacceleratorstorefront directory. I had to delete it manually each time to resolve the build error.

entry.title: Re: clean core alternative to BAPI_OBJCL_CHANGE?
entry.content:
Hi,

There is currently no direct RAP BO or release API equivalent for BAPI_OBJCL_CHANGE.

In S/4 HANA (especially ABAP cloud), classification changes are not exposed via a standard RAP business object like some read-only CDS views are for BAPI_OBJCL_GETDETAIL.

What are the options?

  1. On-prem / Classic ABAP, You can still use: 
    • BAPI_OBJCL_CHANGE
    • Or Function Module like CLAF_CLASSIFICATION_OF_OBJECTS (depend on object type and use case).
  2. ABAP Cloud / Steampunk
    • BAPI_OBJCL_CHANGE is not released for cloud usage. In this case 
      • Use a released API if available (check API HUB)
      • Or build a custom RAP BO that updates classification tables via released objects only (direct update of AUSP/KSSK/INOB is not allowed in Cloud).
  3. Alternative
    • If the object (eg. Material, Batch, Equipment) has a released API for update, sometimes classification can be handled indirectly through that object API-but this depends on the business object.

Summary:

Thers is no standard RAP BO replacement for BAPI_OBJCL_CHANGE at the moment.

In ABAP Cloud, you'll need to check for a released API per object type or implement a compliant wrapper service.

Hope this clarifies.

entry.title: Issue with Updating Operation wise Confirmation Time via RAP Application
entry.content:
Hello Expert,

I am currently working on an SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud RAP application where we have implemented a custom field called “Execution Time” as per the business requirement.

The requirement is to update the execution time operation wise in the standard transaction CO03. For this purpose, we are using EML with I_ProductionOrderConfirmationTP to update the fields OpConfirmedWorkQuantity1 and OpConfirmedWorkQuantity2, which are intended to store the time values.

We have written the necessary logic to update both quantity and time using the same EML statement. However, the changes are not getting reflected as expected in the system.

For your reference, we have also attached a snapshot of the Time field along with the relevant code.

Could you please review and suggest if there is an alternative approach or any correction required to achieve the desired update?

Best Regards,

Thank you,

READ ENTITIES OF i_productionordconfirmationtp
ENTITY productionorderconfirmation
EXECUTE getconfproposal
FROM VALUE #(( confirmationgroup = lv_opconf
             %param-confirmationyieldquantity = <lfs_childbillet>-totalweight
            ) )
        RESULT DATA(lt_confproposal)
        REPORTED DATA(lt_reported_conf).

LOOP AT lt_confproposal ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<ls_confproposal>).
APPEND INITIAL LINE TO lt_confirmation ASSIGNING FIELD SYMBOL(<ls_confirmation>).
       <ls_confirmation>-%cid = 'Conf' && sy-tabix.
       <ls_confirmation>-%data = CORRESPONDING #( <ls_confproposal>-%param ).
       <ls_confirmation>-%data-ConfirmedExecutionStartTime = lv_start_ts.
       <ls_confirmation>-%data-ConfirmedExecutionEndTime   = lv_end_ts.
       <ls_confirmation>-%data-finalconfirmationtype = 'X'.

 READ ENTITIES OF i_productionordconfirmationtp
      ENTITY productionorderconfirmation
      EXECUTE getgdsmvtproposal
FROM VALUE #( ( confirmationgroup = <ls_confproposal>-confirmationgroup
            %param-confirmationyieldquantity =  <lfs_childbillet>-totalweight
            ) )
      RESULT DATA(lt_gdsmvtproposal)
      REPORTED DATA(lt_reported_gdsmvt).
      CHECK lt_gdsmvtproposal[] IS NOT INITIAL.
      CLEAR lt_target[].
      DELETE lt_gdsmvtproposal[] WHERE %param-quantityinentryunit IS INITIAL.

        LOOP AT lt_gdsmvtproposal ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<ls_gdsmvtproposal>)
        WHERE confirmationgroup = <ls_confproposal>-confirmationgroup .
        APPEND INITIAL LINE TO lt_target ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<ls_target>).
       
        <ls_target> = CORRESPONDING #( <ls_gdsmvtproposal>-%param ).
        <ls_target>-%cid = 'Item' && sy-tabix.
        ENDLOOP.

  APPEND VALUE #( %cid_ref = <ls_confirmation>-%cid
                  %target = lt_target
  confirmationgroup = <ls_confproposal>-confirmationgroup ) TO lt_matldocitm.

      ENDLOOP.

      MODIFY ENTITIES OF i_productionordconfirmationtp
      ENTITY productionorderconfirmation
      CREATE FROM lt_confirmation
      CREATE BY \_prodnordconfmatldocitm FROM lt_matldocitm
      MAPPED DATA(poconfirmmapped)
      FAILED DATA(poconfirmfailed)
      REPORTED DATA(poconfirmreported).

    ENDIF.

entry.title: Re: clean core alternative to BAPI_OBJCL_CHANGE?
entry.content:
I can only agree with what has already been said.
There is currently no released objects available for changing classification information.

Depending on your S/4 version there are released CDS views for reading classification, though this does not seem like your goal. These views can be found via the view browser by setting it to show only released views and looking on 'Classification'.
In the systems I have at hand I only find views for getting the characteristics and class details, nothing yet to get actual object classification :(.
However, the fact that there is already something gives me hope that it might come in a next version (thinking 2027 for private/on-prem).

It still remains a challenging puzzle to work clean core. That said this is to me also what keeps things interesting 😁

entry.title: The Path to SAP Cloud ERP Private –Technical Assessment
entry.content:
With SAP Cloud ERP Private, SAP removes the complexity of designing, building, and operating SAP software for customers by offering a user- or metric-based subscription that bundles software and tooling licenses, infrastructure, and technical managed services—everything needed to run an SAP solution. Read more about this here: Understanding the Distinct Scope of SAP Cloud ERP Private

To ensure a smooth transition to the cloud, SAP collaborates with each customer and partner on all design, build, and operational topics relevant to SAP Cloud ERP Private, assessing the technical feasibility of each customer’s requirements and handing them over to ), the business unit within SAP that is responsible for designing, building and operating SAP Cloud ERP Private.

Moving to a cloud subscription service either from an on-premise datacenter or indeed from public cloud IaaS is not a trivial task. It requires due diligence to ensure that all technical and operational requirements are met, and where deviations exist, a solution is achieved to deliver the same business outcome.

To support our customers’ transition to the private cloud, SAP offers a safeguarding process which covers all the technical and operational requirements. This process, which we call Technical Assessment, is delivered by a unit within Global Cloud Operations called Cloud Architecture & Advisory (CAA).

This document explains the role and value of the Technical Assessment process in the journey to SAP Cloud ERP Private. It outlines how SAP works with customers and partners to achieve the following:

  • Advise on suitable private cloud options and high-level architecture choices
  • Assess technical requirements and align expectations across stakeholders
  • Transition the agreed outcomes into a clear and feasible target architecture before delivery

By providing an overview of the assessment domains, decision points, and outcomes, this document aims to give stakeholders a clear understanding of how the Technical Assessment helps ensure a secure, scalable, and successful transition to SAP Cloud ERP Private.

Figure1 : Technical Assessment Flywheel

Advise the customer on the private cloud offerings within the SAP GCO portfolio

A key factor to ensure a successful transition to SAP’s Private Cloud is to understand the private cloud offering and the details of each technical domain such as:

 

Technical Domain

Expected customer’s stakeholder

SAP Cloud ERP Private Overview

All key stakeholders

Cloud Technical Architecture

Infrastructure, application, basis, and network stakeholders

Network and Integration

Infrastructure, network, and security stakeholders

Cybersecurity and Compliance

Security, network, infrastructure, application, and basis stakeholders

Cloud Managed Services and Operating Model

All key stakeholders

Customer Onboarding

Infrastructure, application, basis, and network stakeholders

Table 1: Technical Domains and expected participants

These domains provide a structured lens to assess customer needs and requirements as well as position the right GCO services. Depending on the complexity of the requirements, some topics will require deep‑dive workshops to reach a shared understanding and robust solution options. This collaborative approach enables tailored architectures and operating models that align to the customer’s objectives and proactively address potential challenges during the move to SAP Cloud ERP Private.

Assess the technical feasibility of the customer’s requirements

Figure2: Technical Assessment aspects

Aligning the customer’s current and target (“to‑be”) state is essential to define a clear path for their cloud journey. This assessment ensures both sides share the same expectations on scope, timelines, and responsibilities.

Throughout this process, SAP validates that the customer’s business and operational requirements can be implemented within SAP’s private cloud delivery standards. It also confirms that the solution follows GCO best practices for business continuity, operations, and cybersecurity.

Some aspects to be covered during the assessment are the following:

Aspect

Topics to be discussed

Decisions to be taken

Deployment option, platform, and region

As described in the blog From On-Premise to Cloud: Making the Right SAP ERP Move, SAP offers several deployment options for SAP Cloud ERP Private, such as hyperscaler, SAP data center, customer data center, and premium suppliers – each available on different platforms and in multiple regions. It is the customer’s responsibility to choose the deployment model and select the platform and target regions, with support from the SAP CAA team to align with requirements, synergies, and the customer’s cloud strategy.

  • Deployment option (hyperscaler, SAP data center, customer data center, premium supplier)
  • Cloud provider or hyperscaler for the deployment
  • Regions (main and disaster recovery)

Network and communication infrastructure

Each platform provides different private and internet-facing connectivity options. Based on the chosen deployment option, platform, region, and performance needs, the customer must decide how to connect to SAP Cloud ERP Private, including any requirements for network segregation and DNS integration that should be clarified during the Technical Assessment.

  • Connectivity options for private and public access
  • Bandwidth required
  • DNS integration method (zone transfer, conditional forwarding, etc.)
  • Load balancing requirements

Application platform and integrations

It’s crucial to clarify which bill of materials, applications, and solutions are in scope for the private cloud, how they are offered in the GCO portfolio (for example, high availability, disaster recovery, and SLAs), and how they will integrate with SAP and non‑SAP systems.

  • Bill of material and solution’s versions
  • Business continuity options and SLAs
  • Technical elements required for integration with SAP and non-SAP systems

Solution sizing

Solution sizing from the customer or partner must be translated into cloud technical sizing (for example, database and storage), since accurate sizing is key for a smooth transition and optimal performance.The blog Sizing Your Success: A Guide to SAP Cloud ERP Private is a recommended reference for this activity.

  • Target sizing of the different solutions and tiers
  • Expected growth during the contract’s runtime

Security and regulatory requirements

As explained in the blog RISE with SAP: Comparing the Security of SAP S/4HANA Cloud, private edition Vs SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition, SAP applies a multilayered security strategy and a zero‑trust approach across its cloud services, with controls such as encryption, access control, auditing, compliance, logging, monitoring, and secure operations governed by SAP standards and policies. This underpins the SAP Cloud ERP Private security and regulatory framework. Each organization also has its own security and regulatory needs, and where specific requirements go beyond the standard framework, SAP reviews them and designs appropriate measures.

  • Confirm that SAP’s security framework meets the customer’s requirement in terms of cybersecurity and regulation.
  • Identify any special requirement and find a solution for it.

Best practices and operational model

SAP Cloud ERP Private provides several operating models and SLA levels to suit different customer sizes and requirements. This includes defining backup expectations and reviewing roles and responsibilities (for example, via a RACI matrix) to identify and close any gaps.

-            Operational model

  • Availability SLAs for solutions
  • Customer’s specific backup SLA
  • Review of roles and responsibilities RACI matrix to identify potential gaps

Table 2: Technical aspects to be assessed

The outcome of the technical domain sessions and the customer landscape assessment is a clear and accurate “to‑be” picture of the customer environment, including the transformation strategy. This serves as the blueprint to design and build the final landscape in SAP Cloud ERP Private.

Transition the customer’s requirements to GCO for delivery

SAP Cloud ERP Private is a managed private cloud service delivered by Global Cloud Operations (GCO), requiring a structured handover of customer requirements to SAP.

Early transition planning: Although the formal transition begins after the Cloud ERP Private agreement is signed, preparation should start early to avoid deployment delays that could jeopardize the project timeline.

Support process: The Cloud Architect and Advisory (CAA) team guides customers and partners through this process by collecting onboarding information, coordinating milestones, and ensuring smooth delivery to SAP teams.

The following outlines typical onboarding information to be collected:

Type of Input

Input Needed

Operational


 

  • Support user (S-user)
    Type of deployment (greenfield or brownfield)
  • Planned downtime
    SAP Best Practices activation
  • Financial relevance
  • Customer contact information
  • Regulatory requirements – GxP, HIPPA, data sovereignty
  • Cybersecurity requirements

SAP-specific


 

  • System parameters (SIDs, clients, languages, etc.)
  • Target versions

Network and DNS

  • Connectivity method
  • Connectivity data
  • Private IP addresses ranges
  • DNS information

Table 3: Onboarding information

Outcome of Technical Assessment: baseline and working document

The Technical Assessment process produces the Technical Assessment Report, summarizing all sessions and workshops, the proposed to‑be architecture for SAP Cloud ERP Private, and any assumptions or exceptions from discussions.

This report is shared with the customer for review and sign-off. It serves as the baseline for the commercial contract, delivery onboarding and is a living document throughout the contract lifecycle.

Innovate and grow in SAP Cloud ERP Private

Everything described above represents continuous engagement throughout the entire contract lifecycle, not a one-time effort.

For new customer requirements, the GCO team supports the customer and partner through a renewed technical assessment process. Depending on the requirement, relevant assessment domains and onboarding details will vary, but GCO provides full guidance—advising on goals and value drivers, identifying innovation opportunities, and extending the Cloud ERP Private scope via the change request process.

entry.title: Resolving SAP HANA System Database Refresh Issue – Missing Backup Root Key During Recovery
entry.content:
Introduction

During a recent SAP HANA system database refresh activity, we encountered a critical issue while restoring the Production (PRD) database backup into the Quality (QAS) system. The refresh activity is typically straightforward; however, when database encryption is enabled, additional key management steps are required to ensure a successful recovery.

This blog explains the issue faced, its root cause, and the step-by-step resolution that helped us complete the recovery successfully.

Issue Faced

While recovering the PRD database backup in QAS, the recovery process failed with the following error

 

Root Cause

Our PRD backup was encrypted, as we are running SAP HANA 2.0 SPS07, where backup encryption is enabled by default. However, the required encryption root key was not available in the QAS system. Without this root key, SAP HANA is unable to decrypt or restore the encrypted backup.

Solution

To resolve this issue, we exported the encryption root keys from PRD and imported them into QAS.

Step 1: Set Backup Password for Encryption Root Keys (PRD)

Execute the following command in HANA Studio (PRD system):

ALTER DATABASE <PRD_SID> SET ENCRYPTION ROOT KEYS BACKUP PASSWORD 'MyStrongPassword123!';

This password protects the exported root key backup.

Step 2: Back Up Encryption Root Keys (PRD)

BACKUP ENCRYPTION ROOT KEYS FOR <PRD SID> USING FILE ('backup001');

The encrypted root key file is generated in the following location:

/usr/sap/<PRD_SID>/HDB<Instance_Number>/backup/sec/DB_<PRD_SID>/backup001_rootkeys

Step 3: Copy Root Key to QAS

Securely copy the generated _rootkeys file from the PRD server to the corresponding directory on the QAS system.

Step 4: Recover Encryption Root Keys in QAS

Run the following command from the QAS OS level :

hdbnsutil -recoverRootKeys /usr/sap/<QAS SID>/HDB<Instance Number>/backup/sec/DB_<PRD_SID>/backup001_rootkeys --dbid=4 --password=MyStrongPassword123! --type=ALL

Permissions : 

For the System Database, execution of this statement requires any one of the following system privileges:

BACKUP ADMIN
BACKUP OPERATOR

For a Tenant Database, execution of this statement requires any one of the following system privileges:

DATABASE BACKUP ADMIN
DATABASE BACKUP OPERATOR

Hint : 

How to Find the DBID ?

Run the following query in SAP HANA Studio:

SELECT DATABASE_NAME,

       CASE WHEN (DBID = '' AND DATABASE_NAME = 'SYSTEMDB')

            THEN 1

            WHEN (DBID = '' AND DATABASE_NAME <> 'SYSTEMDB')

            THEN 3

            ELSE TO_INT(DBID)

       END DATABASE_ID

FROM (SELECT DISTINCT DATABASE_NAME,

      SUBSTR_AFTER(SUBPATH,'.') AS DBID

      FROM SYS_DATABASES.M_VOLUMES);

Final Step: Retry Database Recovery

After successfully importing the encryption root keys into QAS, the database recovery was retried and completed successfully without any errors.

 Key Takeaway : 

When performing SAP HANA system refreshes involving encrypted backups, always ensure:

1.Encryption root keys from the source system are backed up

2.The keys are securely transferred and restored in the target system before recovery

3.Please note that from SAP HANA 2.0 SPS 01 version we can create encrypted backup

4.In a newly installed HANA 2.0 SPS07, encryption is enabled by default.

Conclusion : 

System refresh activities in SAP HANA environments require careful handling when encryption is enabled. The missing root key error may seem complex initially, but the resolution is straightforward once the root key export and import process is properly executed.

entry.title: Re: Issues in J62 Asset Accounting – Step 4.1.2 “Create P...
entry.content:
Good day @Wayne10 

Thank you for your question.

Based on the behaviour you described, the errors you are facing are most likely not coming from the standard J62 test script but from a specific control or validation logic in your system.

The Public Cloud only allows you to change field properties for purchase requisitions through BAdIs or Custom Logic (for example PR field control BAdI MM_PUR_S4_PR_FLDCNTRL_SIMPLE) and not via SSCUI. This scenario is addressed in the following: KBA 3268822 and KBA 3345421. The PR check extensibility for custom validation and how to handle error in procurement scenarios is visible in the following reference.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/044700714fc54863b7fa9ea0b5654ba0.html?locale=en-US

So, in your case, this means that a standard J62 free text PR with Asset category assignment account should normally not required Material or Fixed Vendor unless your tenant has an additional rule. Therefore, these mandatory checks usually come from one of the following:

  1. Custom Logic BAdI for PR field control which then makes Material and/or Supplier mandatory. See KBA 3268822 and KBA 3345421 for more details.
  2. Custom PR validation logic that raises hard errors before you save or check it
  3. Source determination or sourcing setup expectation (though this is less likely for a free text J62 PR, but you may consider checking if your process is customised).

The following references provide more details:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/044700714fc54863b7fa9ea0b5654ba0.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/49fb9b57aba49f2de10000000a44147b.html?locale=en-US

To narrow down on the source of the issue, the recommendation is:

  • Reproduce the issue in your PR app.
  • Open Custom Logic and check active implementation in the PR business context, especially BAdI BAdI MM_PUR_S4_PR_FLDCNTRL_SIMPLE. See KBA 3268822 and KBA 3345421 for more details.
  • Review whether any implementation sets Material or Supplier/Fixed Vendor as mandatory for all PR items.
  • Review your PR validation logic for hard error messages that the system triggers if material or vendor is missing.
  • Only if your business process really requires source determination, you may then consider reviewing Manage Source Lists and the relating sourcing setup in your organisation’s procurement process.

You can resolve the issues after those checks by adjusting the custom logic so the mandatory checks become conditional, for example, you can then exclude:

  • Account Assignment Category A
  • Free text PR item
  • The J62 process variant.

That will help you preserve your business controls for other PR scenarios while allowing you to run the standard J62 test step as is intended.

To summarise:

  • The errors you provided in your screenshots are most likely a specific validation related and not standard J62 behaviour.
  • In Public Cloud, PR field control is usually done via Custom Logic BADIs not SSCUI and can make fields mandatory
  • Check you Custom Logic first, then source related setup if you still require it.
  • You can fix the issue by making your system validation conditional for Asset free text PR scenarios.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris
